Overview Request flow HTTP API Storage Roadmap

Request flow

Two flows: push (agent puts bytes in) and serve (human gets bytes out). Both share the same HTTP server.

Push flow

crate push ./site foo
↓ reads ~/.config/crate/config.yaml (token, base URL)
tar stream via io.Pipe (no temp file, no buffering)
↓ PUT /api/sites/foo, Authorization: Bearer ...
crated
↓ extract into .foo.stage-<rand>/, validate paths
sites/.foo.stage-7xq2/
↓ rename(old → .foo.old-<ts>), rename(stage → foo), rm old
sites/foo/
↓ 200 OK { site, url }
prints http://localhost:7777/foo/

The push body is a raw tar stream — no JSON envelope. That keeps the upload streamable (CLI hasn't seen the whole archive when it starts sending) and means no temp file on either side.

Streaming via io.Pipe. On the CLI: one goroutine walks the directory and writes tar entries into the write end; the HTTP client reads from the read end. The first byte goes out before the last file is even opened.

Serve flow

browser — GET /foo/about.html
↓
crated mux
↓ split path, validate "foo" against site name regex
stat sites/foo/ — exists?
↓ path.Clean("/about.html") — reject any escape
sites/foo/about.html
↓ http.ServeFile
200 OK, text/html

The static side reuses stdlib's http.ServeFile, which gives range requests, ETags, and correct content-types for free. The job of the handler is just to map the public URL to a safe filesystem path.

Three URL shapes the server handles

URLResult
/HTML index of deployed sites
/foo302 to /foo/ (so relative links work)
/foo/serves foo/index.html
/foo/bar/baz.cssserves foo/bar/baz.css
/foo/../etc/passwd404 (path traversal blocked by path.Clean)
← overview · next: HTTP API
